[03:14:43] common analysis
[03:14:45] okay so what is principal common
[03:14:46] analysis principle common analysis is a
[03:14:49] method for dimensionality reduction that
[03:14:52] is used to reduce the variables or the
[03:14:54] dimensions of the data by transforming
[03:14:57] large set of features
[03:14:59] large set of features into smaller ones
[03:15:02] that contains most of the information so
[03:15:04] let's see
[03:15:06] you have x1 then you have x2 then you
[03:15:10] have x3 and all the parameters let's say
[03:15:12] x 40 let's say 50 okay so what it will
[03:15:16] common analysis does it ask you a
[03:15:19] component let's say you have given a two
[03:15:20] component so it will try to put it will
[03:15:23] try to put
[03:15:24] most of the information into the first
[03:15:26] component x1 and it will try to put
[03:15:28] let's say p1 and and and in the second
[03:15:31] component most of the most of the
[03:15:32] information in these two components
[03:15:34] because we have say to do
[03:15:36] reduce from 40 dimension to two
[03:15:39] dimensions okay so so it will simply um
[03:15:43] compress that like that or put all the
[03:15:44] information into the first data
[03:15:46] variation first and second if you say it
[03:15:48] is one so it will try to put every with
[03:15:51] most of the information in the first and
[03:15:53] it will remove this all so obviously you
[03:15:55] will lose some of the information from
[03:15:57] the data but it's good to have uh not
[03:16:00] more than dimensions okay i will give a
[03:16:02] tip when you when you have to work on
[03:16:03] principle component analysis
[03:16:07] okay so what is the basic intuition
[03:16:10] basic intuition behind principal common
[03:16:12] analysis is that we have a principal
[03:16:14] components which is a new variable which
[03:16:17] are the new variable like let's say i
[03:16:19] have i given you know p1 and p2 they are
[03:16:22] the new principal components which are
[03:16:25] new variables okay that are constructed
[03:16:28] as a linear combinations of the initial
[03:16:30] variables okay so what what what we do
[03:16:34] uh let's say we have x1 x2
[03:16:38] all the way down to x40 so we just try
[03:16:40] to put in all the first and second so
[03:16:43] it's v it's a hyperbranner two is a
[03:16:45] complex number of accomplish the high
[03:16:47] parameter so let's say we have taken two
[03:16:49] okay so it will try to put most of the
[03:16:51] information into p1 and p2 and these are
[03:16:54] the new vectors or the unit vectors or
[03:16:56] the variables like this all and
[03:17:00] it's just a linear combination of these
[03:17:03] variables okay of the initial variables
[03:17:07] okay you will get to know the through
[03:17:08] visualization what i'm saying and these
[03:17:10] combinations are done in such a way that
[03:17:13] the principal components are
[03:17:14] uncorrelated they are uncorrelated and
[03:17:17] most of the information
[03:17:19] from these variables i mean size branch
[03:17:21] etc will be compressed into the first
[03:17:24] components and so on
[03:17:26] okay and we're projecting each data
[03:17:28] point on t1 onto only the first few
[03:17:31] components to obtain a lower dimensions
[03:17:34] of data please preserving as much as of
